learning_materials.context_from_class: Appeared in text about fortune telling and beliefs

examples.english: You shouldn't believe in such superstitions.
They say it's superstition, but many people believe it.

learning_materials.notes: Related to traditional beliefs and fortune telling

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained fortune telling context

examples.english: I heard this year's fortune is good.
I went to a fortune teller to check my fortune.

learning_materials.notes: Commonly used when talking about fortune telling

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained as person who tells fortunes

examples.korean: 점쟁이가 내년에 좋은 일이 있을 거라고 했어요.
examples.english: The fortune teller said good things will happen next year.
I met a fortune teller at the market.

learning_materials.notes: Person who practices fortune telling

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained historical background from Joseon period

examples.english: I checked my New Year's fortune using Tojeong-bigyeol.
Tojeong-bigyeol is a fortune telling method from the Joseon Dynasty.

learning_materials.notes: Traditional Korean divination method from 1600s

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in context of consulting fortune tellers

examples.english: I want to get counseling about my career path.
I made an appointment for a consultation.

learning_materials.notes: Can be used for various types of consultation

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained as formal way to say 'choose'

examples.english: We selected the best candidate.
We're in the process of selecting this year's book.

learning_materials.notes: More formal than 고르다

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher compared with 선정하다 as more casual

examples.english: Which color should I choose?
Pick good apples and buy them.

learning_materials.notes: Irregular verb - 골라요

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used when talking about life's important decisions

examples.english: Marriage is a major event in life.
Think carefully before deciding on important matters.

learning_materials.notes: Formal term for significant life events

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained with example '바보로 취급하다'

examples.english: Please treat the customer as a VIP.
Why do you treat me like a child?

learning_materials.notes: Often used with -로 particle

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained building houses context

examples.english: We're building a new house.
When was this building built?

learning_materials.notes: Passive form is 지어지다

learning_materials.english: to be built, to be erected
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained as passive form of building

examples.english: A new apartment building was built in our neighborhood.

examples.korean: 그 자리에 큰 쇼핑몰이 들어설 예정이에요.
examples.english: A big shopping mall is planned to be built in that location.

learning_materials.notes: Used when buildings or structures are erected

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher used when describing sheep zodiac characteristics

examples.english: Our dog has a gentle personality.

examples.korean: 순한 사람이라서 화를 잘 안 내요.
examples.english: He's a gentle person so he doesn't get angry easily.

learning_materials.notes: Often used to describe personality traits

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained Chinese zodiac system

examples.english: I'm a tiger in the zodiac.
What's your zodiac sign?

learning_materials.notes: Used with specific animals like 원숭이띠, 양띠

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher identified Jing's zodiac sign

examples.english: They say people born in the year of the monkey are smart.
My son was born in the year of the monkey.

learning_materials.notes: Specific zodiac animal

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher mentioned Jing is sheep zodiac

examples.english: They say people born in the year of the sheep are gentle.
My mother is also born in the year of the sheep.

learning_materials.notes: Associated with gentle characteristics

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher mentioned her own zodiac sign

examples.english: People born in the year of the rabbit are quiet and careful.
I have a friend who was born in the year of the rabbit.

learning_materials.notes: Another zodiac animal

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained as past experience marker with emphasis

examples.english: Korean food was spicier than I thought (from my experience).
That movie was really interesting (I experienced it myself).

learning_materials.notes: Emphasizes personal experience or realization

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in example about Seoul changing

examples.english: People have changed a lot.
The weather suddenly changed.

learning_materials.notes: Common verb for describing change

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained past modifier with 던

examples.english: I miss the house where I used to live as a child.
I went to the neighborhood where I used to live.

learning_materials.notes: Past modifier for ongoing past actions

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in dialogue about student accommodation

examples.english: I lived in a boarding house when I was a university student.
The boarding house lady is kind.

learning_materials.notes: Common accommodation for students

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in context of rent increase

examples.english: The room rent is too expensive.
Room rent goes up every year.

learning_materials.notes: Compound word: 방 (room) + 값 (price)

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used with rent increase context

examples.english: The company raised my salary.
Prices keep going up.

learning_materials.notes: Can be used for prices, salaries, etc.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained as grocery shopping

examples.english: I'm going grocery shopping on the weekend.
I went grocery shopping with my mother.

learning_materials.notes: Common daily activity expression

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in context of finding something troublesome

examples.english: Doing the dishes is bothersome.
Putting on makeup every day is troublesome.

learning_materials.notes: Common adjective for daily annoyances

learning_materials.english: to live, to stay, to spend time
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher corrected to use with location particle 에서

examples.english: I live with my parents.
Where will you spend your vacation?

learning_materials.notes: Used for living situations and spending time

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained meeting someone by chance

examples.english: I met a friend by chance on the street.
I discovered a good restaurant by chance.

learning_materials.notes: Has ㄴ sound that requires tongue position

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained as giving compliments

examples.english: The teacher complimented my presentation.
Please compliment children often.

learning_materials.notes: Positive action of giving praise

learning_materials.english: almost did, was about to do
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained as 'almost did but didn't'

examples.english: I almost missed the bus.
I almost told the secret.

learning_materials.notes: Indicates something almost happened but didn't

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in job interview context

examples.english: I have an interview tomorrow.
I prepared hard for the interview.

learning_materials.notes: Common in job application contexts

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in job interview dialogue

examples.english: I want to work at a broadcasting station.
It's a famous broadcasting station.

learning_materials.notes: Workplace in media industry

learning_materials.context_from_class: Part of job application process

examples.english: The written exam was difficult.
The written exam results came out.

learning_materials.notes: Contrasts with 면접시험 (interview)

learning_materials.english: to pass (an exam), to be accepted
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained passing exams

examples.english: I got accepted to university.
Do you think I can pass the exam?

learning_materials.notes: Opposite is 떨어지다 (to fail)

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained as idiom for very difficult things

examples.english: Getting a job these days is like picking stars.

examples.korean: 그 시험 합격하기가 별 따기라고 들었어요.
examples.english: I heard passing that exam is like picking stars.

learning_materials.notes: Idiom meaning extremely difficult or impossible

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in hiring context

examples.english: How many new employees are you hiring this time?
It's difficult to select good candidates.

learning_materials.notes: Used for hiring or selecting people

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in job application context

examples.english: I applied to that company.
How many places are you planning to apply to?

learning_materials.notes: Common in job/school application contexts

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used with 심하다 to describe intense competition

examples.english: The competition is fierce.
I'm stressed because of the competition.

learning_materials.notes: Often paired with adjectives like 심하다, 치열하다

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained difference from 깊다

examples.english: My cold is severe.
I was late because the traffic jam was severe.

learning_materials.notes: Different from 깊다 (deep) but sometimes overlapping

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in interview anxiety context

examples.english: I made a mistake during the presentation.
Be careful not to make mistakes.

learning_materials.notes: Common concern in test/interview situations

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used with anxiety about making mistakes

examples.english: I'm worried about the exam.
I'm worried about the children.

learning_materials.notes: State of being worried or anxious

learning_materials.english: I heard that..., isn't it true that...?
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained as confirming information heard

examples.english: I heard it's going to rain tomorrow, is that right?
I heard you moved to a new place, is that true?

learning_materials.notes: Used to confirm information heard from others

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained 이다 becomes 라 in this pattern

examples.english: I heard you're a doctor, is that right?
I heard you're Korean, is that true?

learning_materials.notes: Special form when used with 이다 copula

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained personality trait

examples.english: You're too stubborn.
Don't be stubborn.

learning_materials.notes: Often used with 세다 or 강하다

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained -쟁이 suffix for people with strong traits

examples.english: My son is a stubborn person.
He doesn't listen because he's stubborn.

learning_materials.notes: Uses -쟁이 suffix for person with that trait

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher gave as example of -쟁이 suffix

examples.english: You can't trust a liar.
I don't want to be called a liar.

learning_materials.notes: Person who tells lies frequently

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained as person who is mean to others

examples.english: He has no friends because he's mean.
Don't act like a mean person.

learning_materials.notes: Person who acts spitefully toward others

learning_materials.english: I hope so, that would be nice
learning_materials.context_from_class: Used to express hope for someone's success

examples.english: I hope you pass the exam.
I hope the weather is nice.

learning_materials.notes: Polite way to express hope or wishes

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained three uses: simultaneous action, contradiction, confirmation

examples.english: I study while listening to music.
You say you have no money but buy expensive clothes.

learning_materials.notes: Multiple meanings depending on context

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher used in example with 면서

examples.english: I received this month's salary.
When do you receive your salary?

learning_materials.notes: Common expression for getting paid

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher mentioned going on business trip to San Diego

examples.english: I'm going on a business trip next week.
Do you have business trips often?

learning_materials.notes: Work-related travel

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained expressing worry about future events

examples.english: I'm worried it might rain.
I'm worried I might fail the exam.

learning_materials.notes: Pattern for expressing anxiety about possibilities

learning_materials.context_from_class: Used in context of failing exams

examples.english: I failed the exam.
I'm scared I might fail.

learning_materials.notes: Opposite of 붙다 (to pass)

learning_materials.english: to gain weight, to get fat
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher used in worry example

examples.english: I've gained weight recently.
I exercise because I'm worried about gaining weight.

learning_materials.notes: Common concern, especially for diet

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher used in skiing worry example

examples.english: The road was slippery so I fell down.
Be careful not to fall.

learning_materials.notes: Physical action of falling or tripping

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher used in example about not calling late

examples.english: I went quickly because I was worried I'd be late.
I couldn't say it because I was worried they'd get angry.

learning_materials.notes: Pattern for worry about ongoing states

learning_materials.english: I plan to stay at this boarding house
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher corrected to add 에서 particle for location

correction_details.explanation: Need 에서 particle for location where action takes place

examples.english: I'll stay at the hotel.
I'm staying at my friend's house.

learning_materials.notes: 지내다 requires 에서 for location

learning_materials.english: it is a method of Tojeong-bigyeol
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher noted textbook error - should end with 이다 not 입니다 in formal writing

correction_details.explanation: Formal written style should use 이다 ending, not 입니다

examples.english: This is a Korean tradition.
He is an excellent teacher.

learning_materials.notes: Formal written Korean uses 이다 not 입니다

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher corrected from 복잡한다면서요 - adjectives don't take ㄴ다

correction_details.explanation: Adjectives use basic form + 다면서요, not ㄴ다면서요

examples.english: I heard it's difficult, right?
I heard she's pretty, right?

learning_materials.notes: Adjectives keep basic form with 다면서요

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained Jing's birth year in traditional calendar

examples.english: I was born in the year of Sinmi.
Sinmi year is the year of the sheep.

learning_materials.notes: Specific year in 60-year cycle combining 10 heavenly stems and 12 earthly branches

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher taught proper way to say birth year

examples.english: Where were you born?
I was born in 1990.

learning_materials.notes: Passive form of 낳다 (to give birth)

learning_materials.english: four pillars of destiny (traditional fortune telling)
learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained traditional fortune telling method

examples.english: I asked them to read my four pillars of destiny.
They check compatibility using the four pillars of destiny.

learning_materials.notes: Traditional Korean fortune telling based on birth year, month, day, hour

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained part of traditional calendar system

examples.english: Dates are expressed using heavenly stems and earthly branches.
There are ten heavenly stems.

learning_materials.notes: Part of traditional East Asian calendar system

learning_materials.context_from_class: Teacher explained 12 zodiac animals system

examples.english: Earthly branches are also called the twelve branches.
You can know the zodiac sign through earthly branches.

learning_materials.notes: 12 branches corresponding to zodiac animals
